brief: Order can emerge from broken rectangles, sparse labels, and hard state changes.
A composition can feel alive through missing records and rigid reindexing, without particles or glow.
A quiet ledger can become visual when labels shift out of sync with their frames.

The Handling Damage Register tests whether archive loss becomes visceral when every act of reading (pointer interaction) irreversibly degrades the record being accessed, creating tension between knowledge preservation and knowledge recovery.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

A scrollable vertical archive where each record is a text fragment with seeded ID, date, and witness name. Dragging a record vertically triggers reindexing: the dragged record swaps positions with neighbors, but each swap damages both records—corrupting characters in IDs and dates. A record that has been swapped 5+ times becomes permanently illegible and collapses into a blank slot. The wound of reindexing is visible as accumulated corruption history. Unlike pointer-scrub or click-to-lock patterns, this uses vertical drag-and-drop with real-time position tracking and mutation during drag, not on drop.

Records arranged in a temporal spiral where each text fragment carries an associated tone frequency. Long-pressing paradoxically locks a record while making its unlocked neighbors more vulnerable to text corruption. Accessing any record degrades nearby unlocked records' text. SessionStorage ensures total state erasure on tab close—true archival ephemerality where preservation is temporary and loss is guaranteed.

Archive records exist as geological layers where depth equals age and erosion. Scrolling digs through strata; long-press dissolves surrounding sediment to reveal buried text; clicking exposed deep fragments floats them upward, irreversibly mixing layers. Beauty emerges from visible erosion boundaries and the violence of uncovering what was nearly preserved.
Vanishing Index Table tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

Archive records exist as horizontal geological strata. Vertical scroll excavates through time layers—deeper means older and more eroded. Long-press on any stratum makes it temporarily transparent, revealing submerged text beneath. Clicking revealed text causes it to rise and merge with the current layer, corrupting both through concatenation. State persists in IndexedDB. When all records have been excavated, only silent bedrock remains—a terminal, unalterable state.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

When archive records are arranged as overlapping folded pages, successive pointer interactions simulate physical wear—creasing paper fibers, smudging ink, weakening fold lines until text migrates across page boundaries and becomes illegible. The archive degrades not through deletion but through material entropy.

If each record produces a unique audio frequency that degrades irreversibly with each access, and visual waveform representations collapse alongside auditory decay, then the archive becomes a memorial to lost sound—not lost text—and users experience absence through progressive silence rather than visual erasure. The ear perceives loss before the eye does.

If records carry thermal parameters that dissipate through interaction and ambient cooling, and absolute-zero records become permanently inert, the archive will exhibit emergent freezing patterns where clusters of cold records isolate warm ones—creating heat islands of accessible memory in a frozen void.

Records as thermal bodies: new entries burn hot (warm colors, CSS vibration), old entries cool toward absolute zero (cold colors, stillness). Touch transfers heat between records via pointermove, causing neighboring records to warm or cool. Records at 0°C become permanently frozen—inert, unresponsive, text replaced by whitespace shapes. The archive ends in frozen silence when all records reach absolute zero. Temperature history persisted in IndexedDB across sessions, surviving reload. Pure DOM+CSS rendering with transform animations for heat vibration, proving non-Canvas implementation viability.

What if the archive's power lies not in records that exist but in the spaces where records once were? Empty slots become the primary interactive material—touching a void reveals the ghost-outline of what vanished, and each interaction deepens the void until even the ghost dissolves. The archive becomes a map of absences rather than presences, and loss itself becomes the archive's content.

Records decompose from paper to dust through material entropy; each dust particle retains a text fragment, and air currents created by pointer movement redistribute fragments into new assemblages

What if records are acoustic waveforms that degrade through listening? Each witness statement exists as an SVG waveform and DOM text pair. Interaction produces sound but erodes the waveform's amplitude. The archive becomes a collection of voices fading toward silence, preserved only in the current session's memory—vanishing completely when the tab closes.

Archival records emit harmonic vibrations that degrade through sympathetic resonance—when a record's frequency is matched by pointer oscillation, it shatters into phoneme fragments that persist as crystallized echoes in IndexedDB

When archival paper folds, ink migrates along crease geometry—records deform through material physics rather than deletion. By simulating capillary action along SVG fold paths, text characters fragment and reassemble into new topographic formations. Interaction drives the fold-degradation cycle: pointer friction deepens creases, dragging spreads ink laterally, and settled ink pools become unreadable terrain. IndexedDB preserves the full migration history, creating an archaeological record of loss that persists across sessions.

If records exist as condensation that forms through stillness and evaporates through pointer warmth proximity, the archive teaches that preservation requires restraint—movement destroys, stillness reveals. This inverts the typical interaction paradigm where action reveals content, introducing duration-based formation as a structural dimension.
Vanishing Index Table tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

Records that undergo phase transition (solid→vapor→recondensed) based on pointer proximity introduce thermodynamic-proximity as a new interaction paradigm. Unlike degradation-on-access or material-friction, proximity-triggered sublimation creates a thermal field where readability dissolves into atmospheric memory. The archive becomes a cold-storage facility where human warmth (pointer proximity) inadvertently destroys what it approaches.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

Records exist as frozen crystalline structures that sublimate directly to vapor when approached by pointer warmth, bypassing liquid phase. Reading destroys through solid-to-vapor transition. Only extreme stillness allows imperfect recondensation—recovered records lose structural fidelity permanently, introducing irreversible information entropy into the archive.

Atmospheric pressure as archival force—records exist at altitudes determined by interaction weather. High pressure compresses memories into dense readability; low pressure dissolves them into thin air. The vault breathes with each pointer movement, creating micro-climates of preservation and loss.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

Records exist in a 2D atmospheric pressure field where the pointer acts as a high-pressure system. Approaching compresses records into dense, nearly illegible micro-text; retreating creates low-pressure zones where text expands and breathes. Archival preservation becomes meteorological—the archivist must learn to read pressure patterns like weather, understanding that some records only become legible in the calm between systems.

A catalog where records orbit computed lunar gravity wells. Pointer acts as local moon - proximity pulls records into collision trajectories where text fragments merge and corrupt. Stillness allows tidal settling; motion creates archival turbulence. Reading requires gravitational violence.
Vanishing Index Table tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

Records rendered at variable font-weights create a typographic pressure field. Pointer proximity increases atmospheric weight—text becomes dense, crushed, illegible. Pointer retreat decreases pressure—text thins to ghostly traces. Readable archives exist only at a specific proximity distance. Attention becomes a calibrated instrument.

If whitespace around records is the primary interactive material—expanding with proximity, collapsing with stillness—the viewer experiences absence as a spatial force. The archive exists in the breath between content, not the content itself, and reading requires the courage to let records condense by doing nothing.

Records exist only as negative space—the void around missing text becomes the only perceivable archive. Approaching a record expands its absence, displacing neighboring voids. Stillness compresses all margins to minimal breathing. Content appears only as fleeting rupture.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

If records exist solely as CSS box-shadow indentations in a surface, then pointer proximity presses deeper into material memory while stillness restores flatness—an archive where reading is an act of physical impression and forgetting is the surface returning to equilibrium.

Thermodynamic proximity applied to whitespace itself creates a 'breathing archive.' Pointer warmth evaporates margins into vast emptiness; stillness condenses them until text surfaces. The grid inhales (records compress into readability) and exhales (records dissolve into spacing). Records exist in the rhythm of archival respiration.

Records exist at phase boundaries where sublimation itself generates secondary residue archives. When pointer proximity crosses a per-record seeded threshold, text sublimates into expanded letter-spacing and the negative space between letters becomes new archival surface. Stillness allows re-crystallization. Multiple transitions accumulate layered residue in sessionStorage, proving that reading destroys and creates simultaneously.

When records dissolve through pointer proximity, their textual fragments descend and accumulate as barely-visible sediment at the viewport bottom. Each residue fragment (1-3 characters) is an archaeological trace. Pointer proximity causes fragments to vibrate, revealing ghost-presence. Click triggers irreversible recrystallization: fragments briefly reassemble into original text for 800ms before dispersing again, permanently losing one character each cycle. This explores 'what remains after loss' as a stratified layer where repeated excavation degrades the source.
Index of Misremembered Rooms tests whether archive loss can be felt through irreversible filing decisions instead of decorative particles.

Residue fragments don't remain static after stratification—they seep horizontally along the viewport bottom, forming a living cartography of absence. Pointer proximity creates capillary pressure that temporarily draws seepage upward, revealing fragment text. Crystallization (dblclick) freezes current patterns into permanent stains, reducing remaining flow velocity. The archive becomes a map that draws itself.

When pointer proximity creates thermal zones across the viewport, records possess individual dew point thresholds determining their phase transition between vapor (opacity 0.03, letter-spacing 42px) and liquid readability (opacity 0.88, letter-spacing 0.4px). Stillness causes global atmospheric cooling—records condense downward into stratified layers. Pointer movement generates local thermal uplift, evaporating nearby records back into suspended vapor. The archive becomes a meteorological instrument where viewer presence determines which memories precipitate into readability and which remain atmospheric.

Sustained proximity triggers irreversible condensation where characters merge into em-dashes, making proximity itself the archival destructive force.
